segments.title: Skua as a misunderstood Fleet Air Arm dive bomber
segments.summary: A book-review episode frames the Blackburn Skua as a capable but constrained Fleet Air Arm dive bomber shaped by carrier size, air group policy, and Air Ministry resistance.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument around Peter C. Smith's Skua. Dr. Clarke presents the aircraft as more capable than its reputation suggests, but caught between Royal Navy carrier-size tradeoffs, the desire to merge strike roles into later aircraft, and RAF/Air Ministry assumptions that hindered naval dive-bombing requirements.

evidence.note: The opening introduces Smith's Skua book and explains why the Royal Navy favored fighters that could bomb or later combined strike aircraft within smaller armored-carrier air groups.
The dive-bombing sight section shows Royal Navy requirements for moving sea targets being diluted by Air Ministry assumptions and stationary-target development logic.
The closing assessment praises the book's naval aviation value, reflects on larger Illustrious-class air groups with Skuas, and argues Blackburn and naval back-channel development deserve attention.
